---
title: "pU-QTL mapping — binomial GLM model"
author: "XSun"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  workflowr::wflow_html:
    code_folding: hide
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.align = "center", fig.width = 8, fig.height = 5)
```

# Overview

We map genetic effects on pseudouridylation (%pU) with the
count-based model as a starting point: model the raw converted-read counts
as **binomial**, test the genotype effect on the log-odds of the
modification rate, and read significance from the **GLM p-value** — using
permutation to **check that the p-values are calibrated**, then **standard
site-level multiple-testing correction** for QTL mapping.

Sites tested: the **5-fold union (called in ≥ 1 fold)** set,
**`r format(12416, big.mark=",")` sites** (see the 5-fold site-calling
page). Samples: the **50 genotyped cell lines**.

# The model

For a given pU site, let individual $i$ have $y_i$ converted reads out of
$n_i$ total reads ($n_i = C_i + T_i$, the read depth at that site):

$$
y_i \mid p_i \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n_i,\, p_i)
$$
$$
\text{logit}(p_i) \;=\; \beta_0 \;+\; \beta_g\, G_i \;+\; z_i^\top \gamma
$$

- $p_i$ — the site's true modification rate in individual $i$.
- $n_i$ — read depth; enters as the binomial trial count, so **low-depth
  observations automatically carry less weight**.
- $G_i \in \{0,1,2\}$ — genotype of the **test SNP** (a cis variant near the
  site).
- $\beta_g$ — the **genetic effect we test**.
- $z_i$ — covariates (e.g. genotype PCs to correct population structure);
  optional in this first pass.

This is a standard binomial GLM (logit link). It is the simplest member of
the count-model family — no overdispersion parameter, no random effects.

# QTL mapping: per-SNP p-value from the GLM

For each (site, cis-SNP) pair, fit the **full** and **null** models and take
the p-value for the genotype effect $\beta_g$ from a **likelihood-ratio test**
(LRT):

$$
\Lambda \;=\; 2\big(\log\text{Lik}_{\text{full}} - \log\text{Lik}_{\text{null}}\big)
\;\overset{H_0}{\sim}\; \chi^2_1,
\qquad H_0:\ \beta_g = 0
$$

```r
full <- glm(cbind(C, N - C) ~ G + Z, family = binomial)   # Z = M-value PCs
null <- glm(cbind(C, N - C) ~     Z, family = binomial)
p_snp <- anova(null, full, test = "LRT")[["Pr(>Chi)"]][2] # p-value for beta_g
```

<!-- We use the **LRT** rather than the Wald $z$-test — with ~50 cell lines it is -->
<!-- the more accurate small-sample test. (This is the same statistic the LOD -->
<!-- score merely rescales, $\text{LOD} = \Lambda / (2\ln 10)$; we keep it on the -->
<!-- p-value scale that is standard in human genetics rather than the LOD scale -->
<!-- from animal-QTL mapping.) -->

# Significance

We follow the standard human cis-QTL approach — GLM p-value, a permutation
**calibration check**, then two-level multiple-testing correction.

## 1. Permutation as a calibration check

The plain binomial GLM assumes **no overdispersion**. If %pU is overdispersed
across cell lines, the analytic LRT p-values are anti-conservative. So we use
permutation to *audit* the p-values (not to set a threshold):

1. **Permute the genotype vector** $G$ across the 50 cell lines (breaks the
   genotype–phenotype link, preserves depth and phenotype structure); refit;
   record the $\beta_g$ p-value. Repeat 5 times, pooled across sites.
2. Under the null these p-values should be **Uniform$(0,1)$** — check the
   QQ-plot and the genomic-inflation factor
   $\lambda = \text{median}(\chi^2_{\text{obs}}) / 0.455$; we want
   $\lambda \approx 1$.
<!-- 3. If **calibrated**, use the analytic p-values below. If **inflated**, switch -->
<!--    to a **quasi-binomial / beta-binomial** model (or use the permutation -->
<!--    p-values directly) to restore calibration. -->

```r
# null distribution of the GLM p-value under permuted genotype
perm_p <- replicate(n_perm, {
  Gp <- G[sample(length(G))]
  f  <- suppressWarnings(glm(cbind(C, N - C) ~ Gp + Z, family = binomial))
  n0 <- suppressWarnings(glm(cbind(C, N - C) ~      Z, family = binomial))
  anova(n0, f, test = "LRT")[["Pr(>Chi)"]][2]
})
# QQ-plot perm_p vs Uniform(0,1);  lambda = median(qchisq(1 - perm_p, 1)) / qchisq(0.5, 1)
```

## 2. Within-site correction across cis-SNPs (gene level)

Each pU site is tested against many cis-SNPs in its window, which are
correlated by LD. We collapse them to **one p-value per site** with the
**FastQTL / tensorQTL permutation beta-approximation**:

1. Compute the nominal p for every cis-SNP of the site; keep the minimum
   $p_{\min}$.
2. **Permute the phenotype** across cell lines $B$ times; for each permutation
   recompute the min p over the same cis-SNPs → a null distribution of min-p.
3. Fit a **Beta$(a, b)$** to those null min-p values; the site's adjusted
   p-value is the Beta CDF evaluated at the observed $p_{\min}$.

This is the same gene-level correction used in the earlier tensorQTL run, now
wrapped around the binomial GLM.

## 3. Across-site correction (genome-wide)

Apply **Benjamini–Hochberg FDR** (or Storey q-values) to the per-site adjusted
p-values. Sites passing e.g. **FDR < 0.05** are pU-QTL; report the lead SNP
per significant site.

<!-- # Illustrative example (simulated) -->

<!-- A small self-contained simulation of one site's cis-scan, to show the -->
<!-- mechanics concretely. 50 individuals, one site, 50 candidate cis-SNPs, where -->
<!-- only SNP #1 has a true effect. -->

<!-- ```{r sim-setup} -->
<!-- set.seed(1) -->
<!-- n   <- 50            # individuals -->
<!-- M   <- 50            # candidate cis SNPs for this site -->
<!-- depth <- rpois(n, 60) + 10                 # per-individual read depth n_i -->
<!-- G   <- matrix(rbinom(n * M, 2, 0.30), nrow = n)   # genotypes (n x M), MAF ~ 0.3 -->

<!-- # true phenotype: only SNP #1 affects %pU -->
<!-- beta0 <- -1.5; beta_g <- 0.8 -->
<!-- p  <- plogis(beta0 + beta_g * G[, 1]) -->
<!-- y  <- rbinom(n, depth, p)                  # converted reads y_i -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("n = %d individuals, %d candidate SNPs; mean depth = %.0f\n", n, M, mean(depth))) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r lod-fn} -->
<!-- # null log-lik (intercept only) is invariant to individual order -->
<!-- ll_null <- as.numeric(logLik(glm(cbind(y, depth - y) ~ 1, family = binomial))) -->

<!-- lod_snp <- function(g, y, depth, ll0) { -->
<!--   f <- suppressWarnings(glm(cbind(y, depth - y) ~ g, family = binomial)) -->
<!--   (as.numeric(logLik(f)) - ll0) / log(10) -->
<!-- } -->

<!-- obs_lod <- apply(G, 2, lod_snp, y = y, depth = depth, ll0 = ll_null) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("Observed LOD: SNP#1 (true) = %.2f ; max of the other %d = %.2f\n", -->
<!--             obs_lod[1], M - 1, max(obs_lod[-1]))) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r permutation} -->
<!-- set.seed(2) -->
<!-- n_perm <- 500                              # (1000 in the real analysis) -->
<!-- perm_max <- replicate(n_perm, { -->
<!--   idx <- sample(n)                         # permute phenotype vs genotype -->
<!--   yp <- y[idx]; dp <- depth[idx] -->
<!--   ll0p <- as.numeric(logLik(glm(cbind(yp, dp - yp) ~ 1, family = binomial))) -->
<!--   max(apply(G, 2, lod_snp, y = yp, depth = dp, ll0 = ll0p))   # genome-wide max -->
<!-- }) -->
<!-- thr <- quantile(perm_max, 0.95) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("95%% permutation LOD threshold = %.2f\n", thr)) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("SNP#1 observed LOD (%.2f) exceeds threshold: %s\n", -->
<!--             obs_lod[1], obs_lod[1] > thr)) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r sim-plot, fig.height=4.5} -->
<!-- dt <- data.table(snp = seq_len(M), lod = obs_lod, is_causal = c(TRUE, rep(FALSE, M - 1))) -->
<!-- ggplot(dt, aes(x = snp, y = lod, color = is_causal)) + -->
<!--   geom_point(size = 2) + -->
<!--   geom_hline(yintercept = thr, linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") + -->
<!--   annotate("text", x = M * 0.7, y = thr, vjust = -0.5, color = "firebrick", -->
<!--            label = "95% permutation threshold") + -->
<!--   scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "firebrick", `FALSE` = "grey50"), -->
<!--                      labels = c(`TRUE` = "true causal SNP", `FALSE` = "null SNP"), name = NULL) + -->
<!--   labs(x = "candidate cis-SNP", y = "LOD", -->
<!--        title = "Illustrative single-site cis-scan: LOD per SNP vs. permutation threshold") + -->
<!--   theme_bw(base_size = 13) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r perm-null, fig.height=4} -->
<!-- ggplot(data.table(perm_max = perm_max), aes(x = perm_max)) + -->
<!--   geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue") + -->
<!--   geom_vline(xintercept = thr, linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") + -->
<!--   labs(x = "per-permutation genome-wide max LOD", y = "count", -->
<!--        title = "Permutation null of the (genome-wide max) LOD; dashed = 95th percentile") + -->
<!--   theme_bw(base_size = 13) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- The causal SNP's LOD sits well above the permutation threshold, while the -->
<!-- null SNPs stay below it — the procedure recovers the true signal and -->
<!-- controls the genome-wide error rate. -->

# Data 

- **Sites**: 5-fold union, ≥ 1 fold — `r format(12416, big.mark=",")` pU
  sites. (A stricter ≥ k-fold set could be substituted; see the 5-fold
  page https://xsun1229.github.io/pumseq/mRNA_5fold_union_site_calling.html.)
- **Phenotype**: per site, per cell line — converted reads $C$ (= $y$) out
  of total depth $N$ (= $C+T$), from the pooled joint counts.
- **Genotypes**: cis SNPs within a window of each site (e.g. ±5kb, 20kb, 50kb, 100kb), from
  the imputed genotypes of the 50 cell lines.
- **Covariates $z$**: phenotype PCs computed from **M-values** (see below).

# Covariates: M-value phenotype PCs

We follow the approach used in the IMAGE paper (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31651351/), which adjusts for the top
methylation principal components computed from **M-values**.

**How they are computed.** For each site $s$ and cell line $i$, convert the
counts to an M-value — the log-odds of the modification rate, with a
pseudocount so that $y = 0$ or $y = n$ stay finite:

https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/1471-2105-11-587

$$
M_{si} \;=\; \log_2 \frac{y_{si} + \pi_s}{\,n_{si} - y_{si} + (1 - \pi_s)\,},
\qquad
\pi_s \;=\; \frac{\sum_i y_{si}}{\sum_i n_{si}}
$$

**Choice of pseudocount.** A pseudocount acts as a prior, and its *ratio*
sets the value each observation is shrunk toward: an empty cell
($y = n = 0$) gives an implied modification rate of
$\alpha / (\alpha + \beta)$. A fixed $1{:}1$ pseudocount therefore shrinks
every site toward $p = 50\%$ — far too high for pseudouridylation, where
most sites have a *low* modification rate, and the bias is worst exactly
at low depth with few modified reads (where a pseudocount of 1 is large
relative to the actual counts). Instead we use a **site-specific**
pseudocount $(\pi_s,\ 1 - \pi_s)$, where $\pi_s$ is the site's overall
modification rate pooled over all reads across all samples. This shrinks
each sample toward the *site's own mean rate* $\pi_s$ rather than $50\%$
<!-- — an empirical-Bayes $\text{Beta}(\pi_s,\ 1 - \pi_s)$ prior (mean $\pi_s$, -->
<!-- total pseudocount $1$). For example at a site with $\pi_s = 0.05$, a -->
<!-- sample with $0$ modified of $4$ reads gives implied %pU $\approx 1\%$ -->
<!-- (site-informed) instead of $\approx 17\%$ (fixed $1{:}1$). -->

Then:

1. **Standardize each site** across cell lines (centre and scale each row),
   so that no individual site dominates the components.
2. **PCA across individuals** on the standardized M-value matrix.
3. Include the **top $k$ PC scores** (one value per cell line) as the
   covariates $z_i$ in the binomial GLM.

```r
# C, N : sites x cell lines (converted, total)
pi <- rowSums(C) / rowSums(N)                  # per-site rate, pooled over samples
M  <- log2((C + pi) / (N - C + (1 - pi)))      # site-specific pseudocount (pi, 1-pi)
Ms <- t(scale(t(M)))                            # standardize each site
Ms[!is.finite(Ms)] <- 0
pc <- prcomp(t(Ms), center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE)
z  <- pc$x[, 1:k, drop = FALSE]                 # cell lines x k covariates
```

<!-- **Scope.** The M-value (and its pseudocount) is used **only** to compute -->
<!-- the covariate PCs $z_i$. It is *not* the phenotype in the model — the GLM -->
<!-- fits the raw counts directly ($y_i \sim \text{Binomial}(n_i, p_i)$, no -->
<!-- transform, no pseudocount). So this choice affects the covariates, not the -->
<!-- likelihood. -->

<!-- Note the M-value is simply the logit on a $\log_2$ scale -->
<!-- ($M = \text{logit}(p)/\ln 2$), so this is the standard logit/M-value -->
<!-- transform of the proportions; the constant cancels once each site is -->
<!-- standardized. -->

**How many PCs.** Not fixed in advance — we will **sweep $k \in \{3, 5, 7,
10, 15, 20\}$** and choose based on the results, as was done for the
earlier tensorQTL PC sweep.

<!-- **A note on what these do and don't correct.** M-value phenotype PCs absorb -->
<!-- *phenotype-side* variation (technical/batch effects), which mainly buys -->
<!-- power. They do **not** correct genotype-side population structure or -->
<!-- relatedness — in the IMAGE paper that was handled separately by the kinship -->
<!-- random effect. Since this simple binomial GLM has no such random effect, -->
<!-- adding a few **genotype PCs** alongside is worth considering, given the -->
<!-- genotype substructure seen in this cohort. -->

<!-- # Notes -->

<!-- - **Why start here**: the binomial GLM is fast (a plain `glm` per -->
<!--   (site, SNP)), so a genome-wide scan with 1000 permutations is -->
<!--   computationally feasible — unlike a mixed model. -->
<!-- - **Planned extensions** (once the simple model is validated): overdispersion -->
<!--   via a beta-binomial / observation-level random effect; the -->
<!--   `treated − input` contrast via an input offset or a treatment×genotype -->
<!--   interaction; relatedness via a random effect or genotype-PC covariates. -->
<!-- - The example above is simulated purely to illustrate the LOD + permutation -->
<!--   mechanics; the genome-wide run on the real sites is the next step. -->
